title: Unification of Upper and Lower Egypt
summary: Around 3100 BCE the two kingdoms of the Nile valley were welded into a single state under one crown, an act traditionally attributed to a king called Menes and now generally identified with Narmer. The moment inaugurated more than three millennia of pharaonic rule, a centralised god-kingship administered from a new capital at Memphis, and produced the world's earliest large territorial state.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
For centuries before unification the Nile had supported two distinct polities. Upper Egypt was the long ribbon of the river valley to the south, its rulers wearing a tall white crown and associated with the town of Nekhen (Hierakonpolis). Lower Egypt was the broad Delta to the north, whose kings wore a flat red crown. During the late Predynastic Naqada III period (roughly 3200 to 3000 BCE) the southern kingdom pushed steadily northward, absorbing rival centres and extending its trading reach into the Levant.

Writing was still in its infancy, so the political story survives mostly through carved ceremonial objects and later king-lists. The Egyptian priest Manetho, writing in Greek in the third century BCE, said the country was first joined under a king named Menes. Modern Egyptology, working from the seal impressions of Den and Qa'a at Abydos, identifies this figure with the historical king Narmer, though a minority view holds that Menes was actually his successor Hor-Aha. The disagreement is genuine and unresolved.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The Narmer Palette, c. 3100 BCE, showing the king wearing the crowns of Upper and Lower Egypt. Egyptian Museum, Cairo.
image.credit: Unknown ancient Egyptian artist, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The clearest surviving image of the event is the Narmer Palette, a shield-shaped slab of greywacke about 64 centimetres tall, carved in low relief and discovered in 1897 to 1898 by James Quibell and Frederick Green in the temple of Horus at Hierakonpolis. On one face Narmer wears the white crown of Upper Egypt and raises a mace to strike a kneeling northern enemy; on the reverse he wears the red crown of Lower Egypt and inspects rows of decapitated foes. It is the earliest surviving depiction of a single ruler in both crowns.

With unification the capital moved from Thinis in the south to a new foundation at Memphis, near the apex of the Delta, where north and south could be governed as one. A national administration of royal governors was appointed, and divine kingship, in which pharaoh mediated between the gods and the land, was set on the footing it would keep until the Roman conquest three thousand years later. Because so much of this transition happened before continuous written records, dates carry a margin of a century in either direction.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Detail from the Narmer Palette showing the king in the white crown of Upper Egypt smiting a kneeling northern enemy.
image.credit: Unknown ancient Egyptian artist, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
Narmer's reign is now taken as the start of the First Dynasty and of the Early Dynastic Period, which lasted until about 2686 BCE. His successors extended royal power into the Sinai and Nubia, consolidated a professional scribal class, and developed the hieroglyphic script from the pictographic experiments visible on the palette itself.

Within a few centuries the machinery built at unification would raise the pyramids at Saqqara and Giza. The pharaonic model, one god-king, two lands, a bureaucracy of scribes, a state cult, proved so durable that even Alexander the Great and later the Ptolemies found it easier to adopt Egyptian kingship than to replace it. The unification of the Two Lands is, in that sense, the ancestor of every subsequent Egyptian regime.

worldContext.text: In southern Mesopotamia the great city of Uruk had grown to perhaps 25,000 to 50,000 inhabitants spread across roughly 250 hectares, the largest human settlement anywhere on earth. Sumerian scribes in the Eanna precinct of the goddess Inanna were pressing the first proto-cuneiform tablets into clay, mostly for temple accounts of grain, beer, cattle, and rations. Monumental structures such as the Anu Ziggurat and its white-plastered temple defined the city's skyline, raised on a stepped terrace that dominated the flat alluvial plain. Legendary early kings such as Enmerkar and Lugalbanda, later commemorated in Sumerian epic, may correspond to real dynasts of this Jemdet Nasr transitional period. Trade colonies planted by Uruk merchants reached as far north as Habuba Kabira on the middle Euphrates in modern Syria and Godin Tepe in the Zagros, spreading standardised bevel-rim bowls, cylinder seals, and administrative bullae. Elsewhere in the region, Susa on the Iranian plateau was developing its own proto-Elamite script, and along the Levantine coast small Canaanite settlements traded cedar and wine with the Nile Delta. The Uruk expansion, as archaeologists such as Guillermo Algaze have called it, was the first attested long-distance economic system anywhere on earth.

image.caption: The reconstructed remains of the Anu Ziggurat and White Temple at Uruk in southern Iraq, the platform that dominated the city's late fourth-millennium skyline.

worldContext.text: Along the Indus and its tributaries the Ravi phase and the earliest Harappan occupations were laying the ground for what would become the Indus Valley civilisation. Villages at Harappa on the Ravi, Mehrgarh in Baluchistan, and Kot Diji in Sindh were experimenting with standardised mud bricks, wheel-thrown pottery, copper metallurgy, and long-distance trade in lapis lazuli from Badakhshan, carnelian from Gujarat, and turquoise from the Iranian plateau. Mehrgarh had already been continuously occupied for four thousand years and was one of the earliest farming communities anywhere in South Asia, its inhabitants growing wheat, barley, cotton, and dates and firing painted terracotta figurines. The full urban flowering of Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, and Rakhigarhi, with their gridded streets, brick-lined drains, and undeciphered seal script, still lay six or seven centuries in the future, but the constituent elements were already visible in the archaeological record. Contemporary sites in Balochistan such as Rehman Dheri were also being laid out on rough grid plans, and craft villages along the Kachi plain participated in a network that stretched from the Persian Gulf to Central Asia.

image.caption: The excavated ruins of Mohenjo-daro in Sindh, whose mature urban plan grew out of the Early Harappan experiments of the fourth millennium BCE.

worldContext.text: On Salisbury Plain the first stage of Stonehenge, a circular earthwork of ditch and bank enclosing a ring of timber posts and the fifty-six Aubrey Holes, was raised around this time. Across Atlantic Europe, from Brittany to Orkney, Neolithic communities were building passage tombs and stone circles, part of a wave of megalithic construction that had begun a millennium earlier at monuments such as Carnac and Barnenez. The great chambered cairn at Newgrange in the Boyne Valley of Ireland, aligned with the winter solstice sunrise, had recently been completed. At Skara Brae on Orkney a stone-built village of interconnected houses was occupied, sharing pottery styles with the Ness of Brodgar temple complex nearby. In the Balkans and central Europe the Cucuteni-Trypillia culture was building enormous proto-urban settlements of several thousand inhabitants at sites such as Talianki and Nebelivka in modern Ukraine, later burned deliberately and rebuilt on the same footprint. Copper metallurgy, first developed in the region generations earlier, was spreading westward. Writing was still absent everywhere in Europe.

image.caption: The stone circle at Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain, whose earliest earthwork phase belongs to the same century as the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt.

worldContext.text: On the arid Pacific coast of what is now Peru, the Caral or Norte Chico civilisation was taking shape in the Supe, Fortaleza, and Pativilca valleys. Large communal platform mounds and sunken circular plazas at Caral, Aspero, Huaricanga, and Bandurria mark the earliest known complex society in the Americas, sustained by anchovy and sardine fisheries at coastal sites and by irrigated cotton, squash, beans, and chili in the inland valleys. Caral itself, first excavated systematically by the Peruvian archaeologist Ruth Shady from 1994 onward, covers over sixty hectares with six pyramidal platforms, the largest rising eighteen metres above the plain. Notably absent are ceramics, metallurgy, and any evidence of large-scale warfare; the discovery in 2001 of a set of thirty-two condor and pelican bone flutes hints at a ceremonial life organised around music. Elsewhere in the Americas, Mesoamerican populations in the Soconusco region were beginning to cultivate maize alongside squash and beans, and Archaic-period hunter-gatherer societies still occupied most of North America, the Amazon basin, and the Southern Cone.

image.caption: The Piramide Mayor at Caral in the Supe Valley of Peru, principal monument of the earliest known complex society in the Americas.
image.credit: Gisela Xiomara Garcia Almonacid, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In China the late Neolithic Longshan culture along the middle and lower Yellow River and the Liangzhu culture in the lower Yangtze delta were producing fine wheel-thrown black burnished pottery, thin-walled eggshell goblets, and elaborate jade objects, and building rammed-earth walls around proto-urban settlements such as Taosi in Shanxi and Liangzhu itself near modern Hangzhou. Liangzhu, in particular, has yielded evidence of large-scale hydraulic engineering, including an eleven-kilometre network of dams and reservoirs, that some archaeologists such as Colin Renfrew consider the earliest state-level society in China. The characteristic jade cong, a squared tube incised with stylised human-animal faces, and the flat bi disc were produced in enormous numbers for elite burials, testifying to a stratified society with specialist craftsmen and long-distance exchange in nephrite from the Kunlun Mountains. To the north, on the Liaodong peninsula, the Hongshan culture had recently declined. Writing, in the form of the oracle-bone script, would not appear for another two thousand years, though a small number of Liangzhu jades bear incised marks whose status as proto-writing remains disputed.

image.caption: A Liangzhu culture jade cong, third millennium BCE, incised with the culture's characteristic stylised human-animal face motif. Shanghai Museum.
image.credit: Prof. Gary Lee Todd, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Narmer
figures.role: First-Dynasty king of Egypt, most likely the historical figure behind the tradition of 'Menes' the unifier
figures.credit: Narmer Palette (c. 3100 BCE), Egyptian Museum, Cairo; via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Hor-Aha
figures.role: Narmer's successor, identified by a minority of scholars as the true 'Menes' and founder of the united kingdom

figures.name: Manetho
figures.role: Ptolemaic-era Egyptian priest whose Greek-language king-list preserved the name Menes for the first unifier

images.caption: The Narmer Palette, c. 3100 BCE, showing the king wearing the crowns of Upper and Lower Egypt on its two faces. Egyptian Museum, Cairo.
images.credit: Unknown ancient Egyptian artist, via Wikimedia Commons
